difficult to comply with since your office has given us virtu—
ally no guidelines
in terms
of what should or should not be
reported.
These issues were raised in October of 1979 at least
six months prior to the initial campaign reports filed in con-
junction with the Primary Election of 19m.
The purpose of the
letters of October, 1979, and March 1950 was to attempt to get
some information from your office prior to the time when campaign
reports are due during the election period.
I personally can
not think of a more important area that needs to be clarified in
the campaign reporting area and would ask your office to cive

on the reporting issues that have been raised in my earlier

Re:
Advice Regest Xe Government Code Section 64210 h
Dear Ted:
we would appreciate clarification of the reporting
requirements for expenditures made to companies that produce
radio or television spots.
A campaign manager usually presents the committee
with a proposed budget and an advertising plan.
most managers
then write the scripts, have them approved by the client and
then either produce the spots themselves or contract with a
production company.
if Lhe advertising firm produces these
spots,
any payments made by the advertising firm for which
reimbursement is sought from the campaign would be disclosed
Pursuant to Government Code
Section 84303.
However,
if the
advertising firm contracts the production to an independent
production company for a flat fee which is paid by the cam-
paign committee, what disclosure is required?
The expenditures usually made by the production
company are fees to actors and actresses, costs incurred at
the site of the production intluding rental charges, construc»
tion charges, special equipment, room and board, and many
related incidentals such as
taxis,
lunches,
coffee,
etc.
In
the examples given to us,
all these decisions
and costs are
the responsibility of the production company and its profit
is the amount remaining once all costs are paid,
1 have
Been informed that the usual
practice
is
for
the Production
company personnel,
and not the campaiw,
to choose the actors,
actresses, photographers, sound track technicians, etc”
During the last few months, we have encountered
great resistance from two separate production companies when


,
(ti

Ted Prim
W)
to
March )5,
1980
Page 2
we instructed them to disclose to the campaign their expendi-
tures of $100 or more in producing the campaign spots.
The
companies and the campaign managers argue that these companies
are not political organizations and are producing a product
for
a
fixed fee which is sufficient. and complete disclosure.
They argue that no disclosure is required of the component
costs of other products that are purchased by campaigns such
as polling, printing, telephones,
legal, accounting,
etc..
Your assistance and cooperation is sincerely
appreciated.
Very t
ly yol7rs,
W
Vigo G.

Re:
Responsibilities of Campaign Treasurers
Dear Mr. Sahel:
Thank you for your letter of December 14, 1979 in
reply to my correspondence of October 25, 1979.
The purpose of
my letter was to seek better clarification of the duties of a
committee treasurer.
The motivation for this request is our
concern that Regulation 13427, including the comment, does not
seem to provide treasurers with clear and adequate notice of
their responsibilities, particularly in light of the recent
spec lawsuit against a number of committees
(and their campaign
consultant Butcher/Ford) and also. the recent FPPC enforcement
matter against the Quentin Kopp Committee.
In the lawsuit, the
FPPC informs that court that treasurers should know how to
properly disclose expenditures containing commissions and
public relations fees, cvcn though Regulation 13427 focus-cs
almosr totally on contributions and your campaign Disclosure
Manual is virtually silent on this subject.
in the ggpp case,
the rrpc staff is guesticning the diligence of a treasurer even
though the treasurer did question the source of the contribution,
did ask the candidate to obtain a transmittal letter from the
supposed donor to confirm his identity, and did receive the
confirming latter.
The question raised is how far past the invoice must
the treasurer look before paying a bill and what further itemizdr
tion is required on the campaign report other than the payee,
For example,
1) must a treasurer disclose only "pass through"
payments or commissions to people involved in the vendor's
trade (travel agency, etc.) when such commissions have no
benefit to any political persons?
2) Must a rreasurer disclose
subcontracting done by a vendor on the vendor's own initiation
in the regular course of the vendor's busincss if such Subcun‘
tracting payments by the vendor are not itemized on the invoice
Ii.e., a printer who subcontracts any or all of the design,


Thomas M, Sobel, Esq.
Re:
Responsiblities
of Campaign Treasurers
March
107
1950

layout, printing, delivering, etcr of a printing job)?
at
ls
disclosure required of any nonpolitical personnel specifically
hired by a vendor to perform work on the committee's job (ie,
a printer hires an extra assistant for two days to assist the
printing job or a
civil hires an additional bookkeeper during an
election year to help on various campaign accounts)? There are
a number of other variations
of
these
situations which
are
raised by Government Code 5842100!)
and
534303,
It
is clear that the FPYC believes that Certain
payments by the payees are disclosable (payments by advertising
firms
to the media,
etCi)
,
but the FYPC has given virtually no
clarification of its view on what other component payments are
disclosable.
Further,
the Cements
to REQulation 18427 are primarily
oriented to the treasurer's responsibility to question "suspect"
contribution data.
What requirements are imposed on the treasurer
as it relates to expenditures?
Does a treasurer need to require
an affidavit by all vendors to accompany each invoice stating
that the vendor understand the requirements of Government Code
58421001)
and
§BA303
and has
not made
any such payments
as part
of the invoice being submitted?
As mentioned in my October 25.
1979 letter.
our
ofiice would be please to work with you and your staff to
further explain the current confusions,
as well as to work
toward a clarifying regulation or opinion.
Very sincerely,
JOHN H,
HODGSON

October 25, 1979
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1100 K street
Sacramento, California
95814
Attnz
Ted Prim, Executive Director
hear Mr. Prim:
As you are aware, this office represents a number of
political committees as well as serving as treasurer for other
committees.
Because of the recent actions by the Fair Politi-
cal practices Commission, we are writing this letter to seek
clarification of the reporting responsibilities under Govern-—
ment Code 534303 and 5942mm).
it is unclear what the
responsibilities of a treasurer are, particularly as they relate
to the reporting of expenditures under these two sections of the
law.
i would ask that this matter be given immediate attention
because of the confusion that exists,
Our office would be pleased to work with your staff
to explain the current confusion as well as work towards a
clarifying regulation or opinion.
very sincerely,
JOHN H. HODGSON II

sections.question: 1) Must a treasurer disclose only "pass through" payments or commissions to people involved in the vendor's trade (travel agency, etc.) when such commissions have no benefit to any political persons? 2) Must a treasurer disclose subcontracting done by a vendor on the vendor's own initiation in the regular course of the vendor's business if such subcontracting payments by the vendor are not itemized on the invoice (i.e., a printer who subcontracts any or all of the design, layout, printing, delivering, etc. of a printing job)? 3) Is disclosure required of any nonpolitical personnel specifically hired by a vendor to perform work on the committee's job (i.e., a printer hires an extra assistant for two days to assist the printing job or a vendor hires an additional bookkeeper during an election year to help on various campaign accounts)?
sections.conclusion: Section 84303 covers certain types of expenditures, such as payments by an advertising agency hired by a candidate's committee to a newspaper or television station for a political advertisement, or for an independent expert consultant hired to prepare a report on election trends for a candidate. It does not cover expenditures such as routine expenditures made by an entertainment production company hired by an advertising agency in the course of producing a political advertisement for which a script has been written by the advertising agency. The fees to actors and actresses, rental fees, construction of special equipment costs, room, board, and incidental expenses incurred by the production company need not be separately reported as a result of Section 84303; it is sufficient to report the flat fee paid to the production company by the advertising agency. Regarding pass-through payments and commissions, the question was too general to answer without specific fact situations. Regarding subcontracting done by a vendor on its own initiative, items such as layout, printing, or delivery of a final product from the printer would generally not be reportable under Section 84303. Regarding nonpolitical personnel hired by a vendor, such as a painter hiring an assistant, Section 84303 would not require reporting of the assistant's salary, though the answer might differ if an advertising agency hired an independent expert consultant.
sections.question_synthetic: What campaign treasurer reporting obligations exist under Government Code Section 84303 regarding vendor expenditures, including pass-through payments, subcontracting, and hiring of nonpolitical personnel by production companies and other vendors?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC concludes that Section 84303 requires reporting of the flat fee paid to production companies by advertising agencies, but not the component costs incurred by the production company itself (such as actor fees, equipment rental, or incidentals). Pass-through payments, vendor subcontracting in the regular course of business, and nonpolitical personnel hired by vendors generally do not require separate reporting, though the answer depends on specific factual situations involving the nature of the work and types of personnel involved.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple incoming letters from attorneys (March 15, 1980 from Vigo Nielsen regarding production company costs; March 10, 1980 from H. Hodgson regarding treasurer responsibilities; June 1980 follow-up from H. Hodgson) followed by FPPC response letter dated September 12, 1980 from Barbara A. Miliian, Staff Counsel. Some OCR degradation in original document affects readability of certain sections. Multiple related correspondence included showing chronology of requests.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1) Must a treasurer disclose only "pass through" payments or commissions to people involved in the vendor's trade (travel agency, etc.) when such commissions have no benefit to any political persons? 2) Must a treasurer disclose subcontracting done by a vendor on the vendor's own initiation in the regular course of the vendor's business if such subcontracting payments by the vendor are not itemized on the invoice (i.e., a printer who subcontracts any or all of the design, layout, printing, delivering, etc. of a printing job)? 3) Is disclosure required of any nonpolitical personnel specifically hired by a vendor to perform work on the committee's job (i.e., a printer hires an extra assistant for two days to assist the printing job or a vendor hires an additional bookkeeper during an election year to help on various campaign accounts)?

CONCLUSION: Section 84303 covers certain types of expenditures, such as payments by an advertising agency hired by a candidate's committee to a newspaper or television station for a political advertisement, or for an independent expert consultant hired to prepare a report on election trends for a candidate. It does not cover expenditures such as routine expenditures made by an entertainment production company hired by an advertising agency in the course of producing a political advertisement for which a script has been written by the advertising agency. The fees to actors and actresses, rental fees, construction of special equipment costs, room, board, and incidental expenses incurred by the production company need not be separately reported as a result of Section 84303; it is sufficient to report the flat fee paid to the production company by the advertising agency. Regarding pass-through payments and commissions, the question was too general to answer without specific fact situations. Regarding subcontracting done by a vendor on its own initiative, items such as layout, printing, or delivery of a final product from the printer would generally not be reportable under Section 84303. Regarding nonpolitical personnel hired by a vendor, such as a painter hiring an assistant, Section 84303 would not require reporting of the assistant's salary, though the answer might differ if an advertising agency hired an independent expert consultant.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to multiple questions about campaign treasurer reporting obligations under Government Code Section 84303, addressing the disclosure requirements for expenditures to production companies producing radio/television spots, pass-through payments, vendor subcontracting, and hiring of nonpolitical personnel.
